svg.text: LIBRAS
HISTORY — DEAF COMMUNITY IN SOUTH AMERICA
LIBRAS ORIGINS: 1857
1855: Edward Huet, Deaf French teacher, arrives in Brazil
1857: Emperor Pedro II founds Instituto Nacional de Surdos-Mudos (Rio)
Huet brings LSF; mixes with indigenous Brazilian sign languages
Result: LIBRAS (Lingua Brasileira de Sinais) — distinct from Portuguese
LIBRAS AND ORALISM IN BRAZIL
1880 Milan: Brazil adopted oralism in Deaf education (like most countries)
LIBRAS survived in Deaf community despite oralist suppression
1970s-1990s: Brazilian Deaf movement advocated for LIBRAS rights
LEGAL RECOGNITION AND ACADEMIA
2002: Brazil Law 10.436: LIBRAS recognized as official language
2005: Decree 5626: LIBRAS instruction required in education courses
University LIBRAS programs: thousands of hearing students learn LIBRAS
FENEIS (National Federation of Deaf): main Deaf advocacy organization
LSA HISTORY · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
LSA ORIGINS: ALSO 1857
1857: Instituto Nacional de Sordomudos founded in Buenos Aires
Same year as LIBRAS founding (Rio) — remarkable historical coincidence
Spanish and French sign methods brought to Argentina; LSA develops
LSA AND ORALISM
Argentina also adopted oralism post-1880; LSA survived in community
1987: APSA (Asociacion de Padres y Amigos del Sordo) advocates for LSA
Argentina: no federal LSA recognition law as of early 2020s (still pending)
BOTH FOUNDED IN 1857
LIBRAS (Rio, 1857) and LSA (Buenos Aires, 1857): born the same year
Both born from same wave of LSF-influenced Deaf education spreading globally
Laurent Clerc's 1817 voyage created a chain: LSF → ASL → influenced Brazil + Argentina
SOUTH AMERICAN DEAF COMMUNITY
Both communities: strong cultural identity; Deaf clubs, Deaf schools
Both: fighting for legal recognition; LIBRAS won in 2002
Sign language is not spoken through the air alone — it is carried in hands
Passed from generation to generation: the oldest gesture of belonging
PLATE 66d ALT · LIBRAS / LSA · HISTORY
